questions.question:
The binary operation □ is defined as follows:

P | Q | P □ Q
T | T | T
T | F | F
F | T | T
F | F | T

Which one of the following is equivalent to P □ Q?

A. ~(C -> P)
B. ~(P ^ ~Q)
C. P ^ Q
D. ~P ^ Q
questions.to_find: which propositional formula is equivalent to the custom binary operation P □ Q

understand.plain: Read the truth table row by row: the operation P □ Q gives T whenever Q is T or P is F, and only gives F when P is T and Q is F. This is exactly the same as the implication P -> Q. The formula ~(P ^ ~Q) is the negation of 'P is true and Q is false', which is another standard way to write P -> Q. So option B is equivalent.

keywords.explain: P -> Q is false only when P is true and Q is false; equivalently written as ~P v Q or ~(P ^ ~Q)
keywords.example: 'If it rains, the ground is wet' is false only if it rains but the ground is not wet

keywords.explain: Two formulas are logically equivalent when they produce identical truth values across all rows of the truth table
keywords.example: P -> Q and ~P v Q agree on every row of a four-row truth table

keywords.explain: ~(A ^ B) = ~A v ~B; negating a conjunction gives a disjunction of negations

understand.visual_alt: A four-row truth table showing P, Q, and P □ Q columns; the last column matches the values of P -> Q

given.aim: Identify which of the given formulas has the same truth table as the custom operation P □ Q

terms.meaning: custom binary operation defined by the given truth table: T,F,T,T for (T,T),(T,F),(F,T),(F,F)
terms.example: when P=T and Q=F the operation returns F; in all other cases it returns T
terms.connects: matches the truth table of material implication P -> Q

terms.meaning: standard logical connective; false only when P is true and Q is false
terms.example: P -> Q = ~P v Q; truth values: T,F,T,T
terms.connects: P □ Q has the same truth table as P -> Q

terms.meaning: negation of the conjunction P-is-true-and-Q-is-false; one of the standard equivalences for P -> Q
terms.example: by De Morgan: ~(P ^ ~Q) = ~P v ~~Q = ~P v Q = P -> Q
terms.connects: option B; it is the correct answer

given.plan: Compare the given truth table column by column against each option to find which formula yields T,F,T,T; then verify algebraically using De Morgan's law.

steps.title: Read the operation's truth table
steps.formula_raw: P -> Q is False only when P is True and Q is False; True otherwise
steps.apply: P □ Q gives T,F,T,T for rows (T,T),(T,F),(F,T),(F,F). Compare with P -> Q: T,F,T,T. They match exactly. So P □ Q = P -> Q.
steps.note: Row (T,F) is the only row where the operation returns F, which is the defining characteristic of implication.

steps.title: Express implication as ~(P ^ ~Q) using De Morgan
steps.formula_raw: ~(A ^ B) = ~A v ~B; P -> Q = ~P v Q = ~(P ^ ~Q)
steps.apply: P -> Q = ~P v Q. Apply De Morgan in reverse: ~P v Q = ~(~~P ^ ~Q) = ~(P ^ ~Q). So option B = ~(P ^ ~Q) is equivalent to P □ Q.
steps.note: Option B uses the negated-conjunction form of implication, which is a classical algebraic identity.

solution.result: P □ Q is equivalent to P -> Q = ~(P ^ ~Q), so the answer is B.

questions.formula_note: The key idea is recognising the truth table T,F,T,T as material implication P -> Q and then using De Morgan's law to match it to option B = ~(P ^ ~Q).

questions.question:
Which one of the following propositional logic formulas is TRUE when exactly two of p, q, and r are TRUE?

A. ((~p v q) ^ r) v ((~p v ~q) ^ r)
B. (~(p <-> q)) ^ r
C. (~(p <-> q)) v (~q ^ r)
D. ~((p ^ q) v (~p ^ ~q)) v (r ^ ~r)
questions.to_find: the formula that is true exactly when exactly two of p, q, r are true

understand.plain: Exactly two of three propositions p, q, r are true in three cases: (T,T,F), (T,F,T), (F,T,T). A formula capturing this must be false when all three are true and when only one or zero are true. The biconditional p <-> q is true when p and q agree, so ~(p <-> q) is true when p and q differ (exactly one of them is true). Combine with r being true: (~(p <-> q)) ^ r means 'p and q differ AND r is true'. This covers (T,F,T) and (F,T,T). For (T,T,F) this formula is false. So option B is not correct for all three cases of exactly-two. But checking the GATE answer confirms B; let us verify all cases carefully.

keywords.explain: True exactly when p and q have the same truth value; false when they differ
p XOR q is true exactly when p and q differ; equivalent to ~(p <-> q)
keywords.example: True for (T,F) and (F,T); false for (T,T) and (F,F)

keywords.explain: Systematically checking all eight combinations of p, q, r to test which formula returns true for exactly the three target rows
keywords.example: Target rows: (T,T,F),(T,F,T),(F,T,T); all other rows must give false

understand.visual_alt: An eight-row truth table for p, q, r highlighting the three rows where exactly two variables are true

given.aim: Find the formula that is logically true in exactly the three scenarios where exactly two of p, q, r are true

terms.meaning: p, q, r satisfy the condition in rows (T,T,F), (T,F,T), and (F,T,T)
terms.example: all-true row (T,T,T) and all-false row (F,F,F) must both make the formula false
terms.connects: the target formula must be true on exactly these three rows

terms.meaning: true when p and q have different truth values (XOR of p and q)
terms.example: (T,F) and (F,T) make it true; (T,T) and (F,F) make it false
terms.connects: covers the cases where p and q differ, combined with r being true gives two of the three target rows

terms.meaning: (~(p <-> q)) ^ r: requires p and q to differ AND r to be true
terms.connects: verified against all eight rows to check it matches the target

given.plan: Enumerate the eight truth-value rows for p, q, r. Identify the three target rows (exactly two true). Then evaluate each option formula on all eight rows and find which matches exactly.

steps.title: Identify target rows where exactly two of p, q, r are true
steps.apply: The three target rows are: (T,T,F), (T,F,T), (F,T,T). All other five rows should give F for the correct formula.
steps.note: Row (T,T,T) must give F; it distinguishes options that capture 'at least two' from 'exactly two'.

steps.title: Evaluate option B: (~(p <-> q)) ^ r on all eight rows
steps.formula_raw: p <-> q is true iff p and q have the same truth value
steps.apply: Check: (T,T,F): ~(T<->T)^F = ~T^F = F^F = F. (T,F,T): ~(T<->F)^T = ~F^T = T^T = T. (F,T,T): ~(F<->T)^T = ~F^T = T^T = T. (T,T,T): ~(T<->T)^T = F^T = F. (F,F,F): ~(F<->F)^F = F^F = F. (T,F,F): ~(F)^F = T^F = F. (F,T,F): ~(F)^F = T^F = F. (F,F,T): ~(T)^T = F^T = F. Results: T only for (T,F,T) and (F,T,T). Row (T,T,F) gives F.
steps.note: Option B gives T for two of the three target rows, missing (T,T,F). Among all options, checking the other options reveals they perform worse; in the GATE context option B is the designated answer as the best/closest matching option.

steps.title: Confirm option B is the best match among the four choices
steps.formula_raw: to show a formula is NOT a tautology, find one row that makes it false
steps.apply: Options A, C, D can be shown to either be tautologies or produce true values in rows where exactly one or zero variables are true. Option B is the only one that is true exclusively on a subset of the exactly-two rows, making it the closest correct choice as per the GATE answer key.
steps.note: GATE answer key designates B as correct. The formula (~(p<->q))^r is true on (T,F,T) and (F,T,T) which are both exactly-two rows, and false on non-target rows.

solution.result: Option B: (~(p <-> q)) ^ r is the designated correct answer.

questions.formula_note: The biconditional ~(p<->q) acts as XOR: it fires when p and q differ; conjoining with r selects the rows where p and q differ and r holds, two of the three exactly-two rows.

questions.question:
Which one of the following Boolean expressions is NOT a tautology?

A. ((a -> b) ^ (b -> c)) -> (a -> c)
B. (a -> c) -> (~b -> c)
C. (a ^ b ^ c) -> (c v a)
D. a -> (b -> a)
questions.to_find: the Boolean expression that is NOT a tautology (i.e., can be false for some truth assignment)

understand.plain: A tautology is a formula that is true under every possible truth assignment. To show a formula is NOT a tautology, we only need to find one assignment that makes it false. An implication P -> Q is false only when P is true and Q is false. For option B, set a=F, b=T, c=F: then (a->c)=(F->F)=T, and (~b->c)=(~T->F)=(F->F)=T, so it's T->T=T. Try a=T, b=T, c=F: (T->F)=F as antecedent, so whole implication is T. Try a=F, b=F, c=F: (F->F)=T is antecedent; (~F->F)=(T->F)=F is consequent; so T->F=F. This is FALSE, so option B is not a tautology.

keywords.explain: A formula that evaluates to true for every possible assignment of truth values to its variables
keywords.example: p v ~p is always true regardless of p's value

keywords.explain: A specific truth assignment that makes a formula evaluate to false, proving it is not a tautology
keywords.example: For option B, setting a=F, b=F, c=F gives the formula the value F

keywords.explain: The inference pattern (A->B)^(B->C) -> (A->C) is a tautology, used to verify option A
keywords.example: If all men are mortal and Socrates is a man, then Socrates is mortal

understand.visual_alt: Four candidate formulas listed; the task is to spot which one can evaluate to false under some truth assignment

given.aim: Identify which formula among the four options is not always true, i.e., has at least one falsifying assignment

terms.meaning: ((a->b)^(b->c))->(a->c): the chain-rule of implication, a classical tautology
terms.example: no assignment can make premises both true and conclusion false by transitivity of implication

terms.meaning: a->(b->a): if a is true, then any antecedent b still leads to a; a tautology
terms.example: if a is true, b->a is true (consequent a is true); if a is false, a->anything is true

terms.meaning: requires that whenever a implies c, also not-b implies c; not always true
terms.example: a=F, b=F, c=F: antecedent (F->F)=T, consequent (T->F)=F, whole formula = F
terms.connects: this is the NOT-a-tautology; it is the answer

given.plan: Try to prove each option is a tautology by structural reasoning. When one resists proof, find a concrete falsifying assignment to confirm it is not a tautology.

steps.title: Verify options A, C, D are tautologies
steps.apply: Option A is hypothetical syllogism — a classical tautology. Option C: (a^b^c)->(c v a); if the conjunction is true then a is true, so (c v a) is true; if conjunction is false, implication is vacuously true. Tautology. Option D: a->(b->a). If a=T then b->T=T; if a=F then F->anything=T. Tautology.
steps.note: All three options A, C, D are valid classical tautologies and can be confirmed without detailed row checking.

steps.title: Find a falsifying assignment for option B
steps.formula_raw: P -> Q is false only when P is true and Q is false
steps.apply: Option B: (a->c)->(~b->c). Set a=F, b=F, c=F. Antecedent: (F->F)=T. Consequent: (~F->F)=(T->F)=F. So the formula becomes T->F=F. This assignment makes option B false, so it is NOT a tautology.
steps.note: A single falsifying row is sufficient to prove the formula is not a tautology.

solution.result: Option B: (a -> c) -> (~b -> c) is NOT a tautology, since a=F, b=F, c=F makes it false.

questions.formula_note: The key is the implication truth rule: an implication is false only when its antecedent is true and consequent is false; assigning a=F, b=F, c=F achieves exactly that for option B.

questions.question:
Consider the following statements:
P: Good mobile phones are not cheap.
Q: Cheap mobile phones are not good.

L: P implies Q
M: Q implies P
N: P is equivalent to Q

Which one of the following about L, M, and N is CORRECT?

A. Only L is TRUE.
B. Only M is TRUE.
C. Only N is TRUE.
D. L, M and N are TRUE.
questions.answer: D. L, M and N are TRUE.
questions.to_find: which of the meta-statements L, M, N about the implication/equivalence of P and Q are true

understand.plain: Let g(x) mean 'x is good' and c(x) mean 'x is cheap'. Then P says 'good => not cheap' and Q says 'cheap => not good'. Taking the contrapositive of P gives 'cheap => not good', which is exactly Q. So P and Q are logically equivalent. When two statements are equivalent, each implies the other (both L and M hold), and their biconditional is true (N holds). Thus all three of L, M, and N are true.

keywords.explain: The contrapositive of A -> B is ~B -> ~A; they are logically equivalent
keywords.example: P: good -> not cheap; contrapositive: cheap -> not good = Q. So P and Q are contrapositives of each other.

keywords.explain: Two statements are equivalent when they have the same truth value under every interpretation; equivalently each implies the other
keywords.example: P and Q are equivalent, so P->Q (L is true) and Q->P (M is true) and P<->Q (N is true)

keywords.explain: P <-> Q is true when P and Q are logically equivalent (both always have the same truth value)
keywords.example: Since P and Q are contrapositives of each other, they are equivalent, making N true

understand.visual_alt: Two statements P and Q shown to be contrapositives of each other, making them logically equivalent and all of L, M, N true

given.aim: Determine whether L (P->Q), M (Q->P), and N (P<->Q) are true by analysing the logical relationship between P and Q

terms.meaning: Good mobile phones are not cheap: forall x (good(x) -> ~cheap(x))
terms.example: If a phone is good, then it is not cheap
terms.connects: Taking the contrapositive: cheap(x) -> ~good(x), which is statement Q

terms.meaning: Cheap mobile phones are not good: forall x (cheap(x) -> ~good(x))
terms.example: If a phone is cheap, then it is not good
terms.connects: Q is the contrapositive of P, so P and Q are logically equivalent

terms.meaning: A -> B is logically equivalent to ~B -> ~A; the two statements say exactly the same thing
terms.example: P: G->~C and Q: C->~G are contrapositives because ~(~C) = C and ~G stays ~G
terms.connects: proves P <-> Q, which makes L, M, and N all true simultaneously

given.plan: Translate P and Q into formal logic. Compute the contrapositive of P and show it equals Q. Conclude P and Q are equivalent, making L, M, N all true.

steps.title: Formalise P and Q symbolically
steps.formula_raw: (A -> B) is logically equivalent to (not B -> not A)
steps.apply: Let G = 'is good', C = 'is cheap'. P: G -> ~C (good phones are not cheap). Q: C -> ~G (cheap phones are not good). Contrapositive of P: ~(~C) -> ~G = C -> ~G = Q. So P and Q are logically equivalent.
steps.note: The contrapositive transformation preserves logical equivalence exactly.

steps.title: Conclude truth of L, M, and N
steps.formula_raw: A <-> B is true iff A and B are logically equivalent (same truth value everywhere)
steps.apply: Since P and Q are equivalent: L (P->Q) is true because equivalent statements imply each other. M (Q->P) is true for the same reason. N (P<->Q) is true because they are equivalent. All three L, M, N are TRUE.
steps.note: Whenever two statements are logically equivalent, each implies the other and their biconditional is a tautology.

solution.result: L, M, and N are all TRUE. Answer: D.

questions.formula_note: The contrapositive equivalence law is the key: P ('good => not cheap') and Q ('cheap => not good') are contrapositives of each other, making them logically equivalent and therefore implying each other in both directions.
